exercises.title: KV spill to CPU instead of recomputing prefill
exercises.prompt: LMCache offloads KV cache out of GPU HBM to CPU DRAM (then disk). Its value even without shared prefixes: when a GPU runs out of KV slots and must preempt a request, it can restore that request's KV from CPU instead of recomputing prefill. Model both: a preempted request restored from CPU (cheap copy) vs recomputed from scratch (full prefill). Show the time saved.
exercises.hint: Preemption happens when KV slots are full. Without LMCache: restore = recompute full prefill (expensive, ~prompt_len work). With LMCache: restore = copy KV back from CPU DRAM (cheap, bandwidth-bound, much faster than recompute). Show restore time for both.
exercises.solution:
LMCache's most underappreciated value is *not* prefix sharing — it is making preemption cheap. When a GPU runs out of KV slots, it must evict (preempt) a request; LMCache lets it restore that request from CPU instead of recomputing the entire prefill.

```python
def restore_time_ms(prompt_len, from_cpu):
    if from_cpu:
        # copy KV back from CPU DRAM: bandwidth-bound, fast
        kv_bytes = prompt_len * 2 * 1024 * 80    # tokens * K/V * dim * layers (approx)
        return kv_bytes / (16e9) * 1000          # ~16 GB/s PCIe copy
    else:
        # recompute prefill from scratch: compute-bound, slow
        return prompt_len * 0.4                   # ~0.4ms/token prefill

for plen in (1000, 4000):
    recompute = restore_time_ms(plen, from_cpu=False)
    from_cpu = restore_time_ms(plen, from_cpu=True)
    print(f"prompt {plen}: recompute {recompute:.0f}ms vs restore-from-CPU {from_cpu:.1f}ms "
          f"({recompute/from_cpu:.0f}x faster)")
```

Output (approximately):

```
prompt 1000: recompute 400ms vs restore-from-CPU 10.2ms (39x faster)
prompt 4000: recompute 1600ms vs restore-from-CPU 41.0ms (39x faster)
```

**Why restore-from-CPU beats recompute.** When a preempted request resumes, the naive path recomputes its entire prefill — paying the full prompt-length compute cost again (400ms for a 1000-token prompt). LMCache instead *copied the KV to CPU DRAM* when it preempted, so resuming is a cheap PCIe copy back (~10ms) — ~39x faster. Crucially, this helps *even with no shared prefixes between requests*: it is purely about not throwing away KV you already computed when memory pressure forces eviction. The vLLM 0.11.0 KV Offloading Connector (Jan 2026) makes this asynchronous and pluggable, so the offload latency is not user-facing. The next exercise puts this in the full production-stack context.

exercises.title: When KV exceeds HBM — the offload break-even
exercises.prompt: Published benchmarks (16x H100) show: when KV cache exceeds HBM, CPU offload and LMCache substantially improve throughput; at low KV footprint, all configs match baseline with small overhead. Build a model of throughput vs KV footprint that captures this: below HBM capacity, offload adds small overhead (slight loss); above it, offload avoids the cliff where requests get rejected/recomputed. Show the crossover where offload starts winning.
exercises.hint: Below HBM: baseline throughput; offload adds ~5% overhead (slight loss). Above HBM: baseline must reject or recompute (throughput cliff); offload spills to CPU and keeps serving (throughput stays high). Sweep KV footprint; show offload loses slightly below HBM and wins big above it.
exercises.solution:
KV offloading is not a free win — it adds small overhead when you do not need it and large benefit when you do. The break-even is whether your KV footprint exceeds HBM.

```python
def throughput(kv_footprint_gb, hbm_gb, offload):
    if kv_footprint_gb <= hbm_gb:
        # fits in HBM: offload just adds a little overhead
        return 1.0 * (0.95 if offload else 1.0)
    # exceeds HBM:
    if offload:
        return 0.85          # spill to CPU, keep serving at reduced but high throughput
    # no offload: must preempt/recompute or reject -> throughput cliff
    overflow = kv_footprint_gb - hbm_gb
    return max(0.2, 1.0 - overflow / hbm_gb)

hbm = 80
print(f"{'KV footprint':>12} {'baseline':>9} {'offload':>8}  winner")
for kv in (40, 70, 90, 120, 160):
    base = throughput(kv, hbm, offload=False)
    off = throughput(kv, hbm, offload=True)
    print(f"{kv:>9} GB {base:9.2f} {off:8.2f}  {'offload' if off > base else 'baseline'}")
```

Output (approximately):

```
KV footprint  baseline  offload  winner
       40 GB      1.00     0.95  baseline
       70 GB      1.00     0.95  baseline
       90 GB      0.88     0.85  baseline
      120 GB      0.50     0.85  offload
      160 GB      0.20     0.85  offload
```

**The break-even is HBM capacity.** Below HBM (40-70 GB on an 80 GB GPU), everything fits, so offload's ~5% overhead makes it slightly *worse* than baseline — there is nothing to spill, so the offload machinery is pure cost. Just above HBM (90 GB), baseline starts degrading (preemption/recompute) but offload's overhead still roughly matches. Well above HBM (120-160 GB), baseline falls off a *cliff* — it must constantly preempt and recompute, or reject requests — while offload spills the excess KV to CPU and holds throughput at ~0.85. This matches the published 16x H100 result: at low KV footprint all configs match baseline (small overhead); when KV exceeds HBM, offload substantially improves throughput.

**The operational implication.** Enable KV offloading when your *peak* KV footprint exceeds HBM — i.e. long-context, high-concurrency workloads where the KV cache (Lesson 09's forgotten 10-30 GB) blows past GPU memory. For workloads that comfortably fit in HBM, offload is a small net loss and not worth the complexity. As with every optimization in this phase, it is conditional: measure your KV footprint distribution and enable offload only where the peak exceeds HBM. The final exercise assembles the full production stack.

exercises.title: Compose the vLLM production-stack layers
exercises.prompt: The vLLM production-stack is the reference Kubernetes deployment: router + engines + KV offload (LMCache) + observability. Build a layered model where a request flows through each layer, and show how the layers compose to handle a realistic scenario: a request routes to the engine holding its prefix (router), the engine serves it (with KV offload absorbing memory pressure), and observability records the trace. Then show a failure (engine OOM) being absorbed by the offload layer rather than failing the request.
exercises.hint: Layers: router (prefix-aware, Lesson 11) -> engine (vLLM, Lesson 04) -> KV offload (LMCache spills under pressure) -> observability (trace). Model a request flowing through. Then inject memory pressure: without offload the engine OOMs and fails; with offload the request is preserved via CPU spill. Show the layer absorbing the failure.
exercises.solution:
The production-stack is not one component — it is layers that each handle a distinct concern and compose to make serving robust. Modeling the request flow shows how the offload layer turns what would be a failure into a degraded-but-served request.

```python
class ProductionStack:
    def __init__(self, hbm_slots=100):
        self.engines = {'e0': set(), 'e1': set()}   # engine -> resident prefixes
        self.hbm_slots = hbm_slots
        self.used_slots = 0
        self.cpu_offloaded = 0
        self.traces = []

    def serve(self, prefix, kv_slots, offload_enabled):
        # LAYER 1 router: prefix-aware routing (Lesson 11)
        engine = next((e for e, p in self.engines.items() if prefix in p), 'e0')
        hit = prefix in self.engines[engine]
        self.engines[engine].add(prefix)
        # LAYER 2 engine + LAYER 3 KV offload: absorb memory pressure
        if self.used_slots + kv_slots > self.hbm_slots:
            if offload_enabled:
                self.cpu_offloaded += kv_slots          # spill to CPU, request survives
                status = 'served (KV offloaded to CPU)'
            else:
                status = 'FAILED (engine OOM)'           # no offload -> request dropped
        else:
            self.used_slots += kv_slots
            status = f"served ({'cache hit' if hit else 'miss'})"
        # LAYER 4 observability: record the trace
        self.traces.append({'prefix': prefix, 'engine': engine, 'status': status})
        return status

# fill HBM, then a request that would OOM
for off in (False, True):
    stack = ProductionStack(hbm_slots=100)
    stack.serve('sysA', 60, off)
    result = stack.serve('sysB', 60, off)   # 60+60 > 100 -> memory pressure
    print(f"offload={off}: second request -> {result}")
```

Output:

```
offload=False: second request -> FAILED (engine OOM)
offload=True: second request -> served (KV offloaded to CPU)
```

**The layers compose.** A request flows through four layers: the **router** sends it to the engine holding its prefix (cache locality, Lesson 11); the **engine** (vLLM with PagedAttention/continuous batching, Lesson 04) serves it; the **KV offload** layer (LMCache) absorbs memory pressure by spilling to CPU; and **observability** records the trace. Each layer owns one concern, and together they form the reference Kubernetes deployment. The toy shows the critical interaction: when the second request would exceed HBM (60+60 > 100), the offload layer is the difference between *FAILED (OOM)* and *served (KV offloaded to CPU)*. The request survives because a lower layer absorbed a failure a higher layer could not.

**Why this layered structure matters.** Without the offload layer, a memory spike fails requests — a user-visible outage. With it, the spike is absorbed transparently (offload latency is not user-facing per the async Connector API) and the request is served, just from CPU-resident KV. This is the same defense-in-depth pattern as the rest of the phase: each layer handles failures the others cannot, so a problem at one level (memory pressure) is caught at another (offload) rather than propagating to the user. The router handles *locality*, the engine handles *throughput*, the offload handles *memory pressure*, and observability handles *visibility* — and you need all four for a production deployment, because each addresses a failure mode the others do not.

**The production-stack synthesis.** LMCache makes preemption cheap (restore-from-CPU vs recompute, Exercise 1), wins on throughput specifically when KV exceeds HBM (Exercise 2), and slots into the production-stack as the memory-pressure-absorbing layer that turns OOM failures into degraded-but-served requests (this exercise). The vLLM production-stack wires router + engines + offload + observability into the reference deployment — and the engineering lesson is that production serving is *layered*, each layer a separate concern with its own failure absorption, not a monolithic 'run vLLM and hope'. Enable offload where KV exceeds HBM, deploy the full stack for resilience, and instrument every layer so you can see which one absorbed what.
